+      "requirement_refs": ["VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7743"],
+      "priority": "mandatory"

+**Traceability link extraction:** Test case documents typically reference specific requirement IDs. These references are extracted to create `tested_by` / `tests` edges in the knowledge graph. If explicit requirement ID references are absent, LLM-based matching between test case descriptions and requirement text is used as a fallback.
+### 5.4 Cross-Reference Extraction
+**Objective:** Identify all references from each requirement to other requirements, other plans (within the same MNO), and external standards.
**Three extraction methods, in order of reliability:**
**Method 1: Explicit Plan References (Deterministic)**
-- Pattern match for `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}` IDs referencing other plans
-- Pattern match for known plan names (e.g., "LTE_SMS", "LTE_Data_Retry") in requirement text
+- Pattern match for MNO-specific requirement ID patterns (e.g., `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}` for VZW)
+- Pattern match for known plan names within the same MNO
- Reliability: High
**Method 2: Standards References (Deterministic)**
- Regex extraction of 3GPP spec citations: `3GPP TS \d+\.\d+`, section numbers, release versions
- Regex for GSMA/OMA spec citations
+- **Release resolution:** If a reference doesn't specify a standards release, use the document-level `referenced_standards_releases` mapping (from Section 5.2) to resolve the correct version
- Reliability: High
**Method 3: Concept/Feature References (LLM-Assisted)**
- For each requirement, LLM extracts telecom concept tags (timers, procedures, interfaces, cause codes, device modes, protocols)
- Requirements across documents sharing concept tags are candidate cross-document links
+- **This operates within a single MNO+release scope first**, then extends to cross-MNO concept linking via the shared feature taxonomy
- Reliability: Medium — requires validation
**Output:** A cross-reference manifest per document listing all outbound references with their types.

-**Step 3: Classify the relationship between each VZW requirement and its referenced standards section:**
+**Step 3: Classify the relationship between each MNO requirement and its referenced standards section:**
Using the LLM during ingestion:
| Relationship | Meaning | Detection Signal |
-| **DEFER** | VZW says "follow 3GPP" with no modifications | "shall follow the steps detailed in 3GPP...", "per 3GPP TS..." with no additional constraints |
-| **CONSTRAIN** | VZW narrows an optional behavior or makes it mandatory | "shall implement X on a per-PLMN basis" where 3GPP leaves this as implementation choice |
-| **OVERRIDE** | VZW specifies behavior that differs from 3GPP default | Explicit timer values, specific parameter settings that differ from 3GPP defaults |
-| **EXTEND** | VZW adds requirements beyond what 3GPP specifies | Requirements with no corresponding 3GPP section, VZW-specific scenarios |
+| **DEFER** | MNO says "follow 3GPP" with no modifications | "shall follow the steps detailed in 3GPP...", "per 3GPP TS..." with no additional constraints |
+| **CONSTRAIN** | MNO narrows an optional behavior or makes it mandatory | "shall implement X on a per-PLMN basis" where 3GPP leaves this as implementation choice |
+| **OVERRIDE** | MNO specifies behavior that differs from 3GPP default | Explicit timer values, specific parameter settings that differ from 3GPP defaults |
+| **EXTEND** | MNO adds requirements beyond what 3GPP specifies | Requirements with no corresponding 3GPP section, MNO-specific scenarios |
**Step 4: Store the delta summary as edge metadata**

The LLM produces a concise summary of what specifically differs (e.g., "VZW mandates T3402 on per-PLMN basis; 3GPP leaves as implementation choice"). This is pre-computed so that "how does VZW differ from 3GPP?" queries can be answered without re-analysis at query time.

+### 5.8 Vector Store Construction

-**Chunk strategy:** Each requirement node becomes one chunk. This is a critical design choice — we do NOT use arbitrary fixed-size chunking. The structural parser has already produced semantically meaningful units (individual requirements).
+**Objective:** Create embeddings for requirement and test case nodes for use in targeted vector retrieval, in a single unified vector store with metadata filters.

+**Chunk strategy:** Each requirement node and each test case node becomes one chunk. This is a critical design choice — we do NOT use arbitrary fixed-size chunking. The structural parser has already produced semantically meaningful units.
+**Metadata per chunk:**
+| Field | Example | Purpose |
+| `mno` | "VZW" | Filter by MNO |
+| `release` | "2026_Feb" | Filter by release |
+| `doc_type` | "requirement" or "testcase" | Filter by document type |
+| `plan_id` | "LTEDATARETRY" | Filter by plan |
+| `req_id` or `test_id` | "VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7748" | Unique identifier |
+| `feature_ids` | ["DATA_RETRY", "EMM_PROCEDURES"] | Feature membership |
**Contextualization:** Before embedding, each chunk is prepended with its structural context:
-[Plan: LTE_Data_Retry | Version: 39 | Feb 2026]
-[Path: SCENARIOS > EMM SPECIFIC PROCEDURES > ATTACH REQUEST > ATTACH REJECT WITH EMM CAUSE CODE > EMM Cause Codes 3, 6, 7, 8]
+[MNO: VZW | Release: Feb 2026 | Plan: LTE_Data_Retry | Version: 39]
+[Path: SCENARIOS > EMM SPECIFIC PROCEDURES > ATTACH REQUEST > ATTACH REJECT > EMM Cause Codes 3, 6, 7, 8]
[Req ID: VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7748]
<requirement text>
-This follows the principle from Anthropic's Contextual Retrieval research — adding document-level and structural context to each chunk before embedding improves retrieval accuracy.
+For test case chunks:
+[MNO: VZW | Release: Feb 2026 | Test Plan: LTE_Data_Retry_TC]
+[Test ID: TC_LTEDATARETRY_001 | Tests: VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7743]
+<test case description, steps, expected result>

| Node Type | Description | Key Attributes | Source |
-| **Requirement** | An individual requirement from an MNO document | `req_id`, `plan_id`, `section_number`, `title`, `text`, `tables`, `hierarchy_path` | Structural parser |
-| **Plan** | An MNO requirement document | `plan_id`, `plan_name`, `version`, `release_date`, `mno` | Document metadata |
-| **Standard_Section** | A specific section of a 3GPP/GSMA/OMA standard | `spec_id` (e.g., "3GPP_TS_24.301"), `section`, `release`, `text` | Standards ingestion |
-| **Feature** | A logical telecom capability spanning multiple documents | `feature_id`, `name`, `description`, `keywords` | Taxonomy derivation |
-| **Test_Case** | A test case from a test plan document | `test_id`, `plan_id`, `description`, `steps`, `expected_result` | Test plan parser |
+| **MNO** | A mobile network operator | `mno_id`, `name` | Configuration |
+| **Release** | A quarterly requirement release for an MNO | `mno`, `release_id`, `release_date`, `is_latest` | Folder structure |
+| **Plan** | An MNO requirement document | `plan_id`, `plan_name`, `version`, `release_date`, `mno`, `release` | Document metadata |
+| **Requirement** | An individual requirement from an MNO document | `req_id`, `plan_id`, `mno`, `release`, `section_number`, `title`, `text`, `tables`, `hierarchy_path` | Structural parser |
+| **Test_Plan** | An MNO test case document | `test_plan_id`, `corresponding_req_plan`, `mno`, `release` | Test case parser |
+| **Test_Case** | A test case from a test plan document | `test_id`, `test_plan_id`, `mno`, `release`, `name`, `description`, `steps`, `expected_result`, `priority` | Test case parser |
+| **Standard_Section** | A specific section of a 3GPP/GSMA/OMA standard at a specific release | `spec_id`, `section`, `release`, `text` | Standards ingestion |
+| **Feature** | A logical telecom capability spanning multiple documents and MNOs | `feature_id`, `name`, `description`, `keywords`, `mno_coverage` | Taxonomy derivation |
### 6.2 Edge Types
+#### Organizational Edges
+| Edge Type | From → To | Meaning |
+| `has_release` | MNO → Release | This MNO has this release |
+| `contains_plan` | Release → Plan | This release contains this plan |
+| `contains_test_plan` | Release → Test_Plan | This release contains this test plan |
#### Within-Document Edges
| Edge Type | From → To | Meaning |
| `parent_of` | Requirement → Requirement | Hierarchical containment (section 1.4.3 contains 1.4.3.1) |
| `belongs_to` | Requirement → Plan | Requirement is part of this plan document |
+| `belongs_to` | Test_Case → Test_Plan | Test case is part of this test plan |
-#### Cross-Document Edges
+#### Cross-Document Edges (within same MNO+release)
| Edge Type | From → To | Meaning | Detection |
| `tested_by` | Requirement → Test_Case | This test case validates this requirement |
| `tests` | Test_Case → Requirement | Inverse of above |
+| `test_plan_for` | Test_Plan → Plan | This test plan corresponds to this requirement plan |
+#### Cross-MNO Edges (implicit via shared nodes)
+Cross-MNO relationships are **not modeled as direct edges** between requirements of different MNOs. Instead, they are realized through shared Feature and Standard_Section nodes:
+VZW Requirement ──maps_to──> [IMS_REGISTRATION] <──maps_to── TMO Requirement
+VZW Requirement ──defers_to──> [3GPP_24.301_5.5.1] <──constrains── TMO Requirement
+This means a cross-MNO comparison query traverses: MNO_A requirement → shared feature/standard → MNO_B requirement. No explicit cross-MNO edges are needed.
+#### Cross-Release Edges
+| Edge Type | From → To | Meaning | Attributes |
+| `version_of` | Requirement → Requirement (different release) | Same requirement across releases | `change_type`: added/modified/removed/unchanged |
+| `succeeds` | Release → Release | This release is the successor of the previous | — |
+**`version_of` edge construction:** During ingestion of a new release, for each requirement in the new release:
+1. Match by requirement ID to the previous release (primary — IDs are typically stable across versions)
+2. If IDs changed (rare), fall back to section number + title text similarity matching
+3. Compare requirement text to classify as `added`, `modified`, `removed`, or `unchanged`
### 6.3 Cross-Document Handling
The graph handles two distinct cross-document patterns:
A requirement in one document is functionally incomplete without understanding a requirement in another document.
-**Handled via typed cross-document edges.** The `depends_on`, `conditional_on` edges directly link dependent requirements. Graph traversal follows these edges at query time.
+**Handled via typed cross-document edges.** The `depends_on` edges directly link dependent requirements. Graph traversal follows these edges at query time.
VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_41013 ──depends_on──> VZ_REQ_LTESMS_XXXX
(Attach Reject behavior)                (SMS over IMS support)
+### 6.4 Cross-MNO Handling
+Cross-MNO queries are enabled through **shared Feature and Standards nodes**:
+Query: "Compare IMS registration requirements of VZW and TMO"
+                    │ IMS_REGISTRATION │  ← Shared Feature Node
+        │ VZW IMS  │  │ TMO IMS  │  │ ATT IMS  │
+        │ reqs     │  │ reqs     │  │ reqs     │
+For standards-based comparison:
+Query: "How do VZW and TMO differ in their T3402 handling vs 3GPP?"
+VZW_REQ_7743 ──constrains──> [3GPP_24.301_T3402_R10]
+TMO_REQ_XXXX ──overrides───> [3GPP_24.301_T3402_R15]
+The graph reveals: VZW constrains (narrows) while TMO overrides (changes),
+and they reference different 3GPP releases.
+### 6.5 Cross-Release Handling
+Version diff queries traverse `version_of` edges:
+Query: "What changed in VZW eSIM requirements from Oct 2025 to Feb 2026?"
+VZW/2025_Oct/LTEESIM reqs ──version_of──> VZW/2026_Feb/LTEESIM reqs
+                             (per requirement, with change_type metadata)
+Pipeline:
+1. Find all LTEESIM requirements in both releases
+2. Follow version_of edges to identify added/modified/removed
+3. For modified requirements, present the delta

+**Default release resolution:** When a query doesn't specify a release, the system defaults to the latest release for each MNO. The `is_latest` flag on Release nodes enables this. When a new release is ingested, the previous release's `is_latest` flag is cleared.

-### 7.2 Graph Scoping
+### 7.2 MNO and Release Resolution
-**Objective:** Use the knowledge graph to identify a candidate set of requirement nodes that are potentially relevant, spanning multiple documents as needed.
+**Objective:** Resolve which MNO(s) and release(s) the query targets, applying defaults where unspecified.
+**Resolution rules:**
+| User specifies | Resolution |
+| MNO + release | Use exactly as specified |
+| MNO only, no release | Default to latest release for that MNO (`is_latest = true`) |
+| No MNO, no release | **Ambiguous** — either ask for clarification, or search across all MNOs (latest releases) depending on query type |
+| Two MNOs (comparison) | Use specified MNOs; default each to latest release if not specified |
+| Two releases (version diff) | Must specify or infer the MNO; use specified releases |
+| "all MNOs" | Search across all MNOs, latest releases |
+**Output:** Resolved scope:
+```json
+  "scoped_mnos": [
+    {"mno": "VZW", "release": "2026_Feb"},
+    {"mno": "TMO", "release": "2026_Q1"}
+  "query_type": "cross_mno_comparison"
+### 7.3 Graph Scoping
+**Objective:** Use the knowledge graph to identify a candidate set of nodes that are potentially relevant, respecting MNO/release scope and spanning multiple documents as needed.
**Process:**
-1. **Entity lookup:** Match extracted entities directly to graph nodes (requirement IDs, plan names, timer names, etc.)
+1. **Entity lookup (scoped):** Match extracted entities to graph nodes, filtered by resolved MNO(s) and release(s)
-2. **Feature lookup:** Match extracted concepts to feature nodes, then follow `maps_to` edges to find all contributing requirements
+2. **Feature lookup:** Match extracted concepts to feature nodes, then follow `maps_to` edges — filtered by the resolved MNO(s) and release(s) — to find contributing requirements
-3. **Edge traversal:** From the initial nodes found in steps 1–2, traverse edges:
-   - `depends_on` → pull in dependent requirements from other documents
+3. **Edge traversal (scoped):** From the initial nodes found in steps 1–2:
+   - `depends_on` → pull in dependent requirements (same MNO+release)
- `defers_to` / `constrains` / `overrides` → pull in referenced standards sections
-   - `parent_of` (upward) → include parent context for structural understanding
-   - `shared_standard` → find requirements in other docs referencing the same standard section
-   - `tested_by` → include test cases if the query is traceability-related
+   - `parent_of` (upward) → include parent context
+   - `shared_standard` → find related requirements (same MNO+release, or cross-MNO if comparison query)
+   - `tested_by` → include test cases if query involves traceability or test cases
+   - `version_of` → include previous/next version requirements if query is a version diff
-4. **Scope limiting:** Apply configurable traversal depth limits to prevent explosion. Default: 2 hops from initial nodes.
+4. **Cross-MNO expansion (for comparison queries only):** If the query is a cross-MNO comparison, traverse shared Feature/Standards nodes to pull requirements from the second MNO
-**Output:** A candidate set of requirement nodes (typically 20–100) with their associated standards sections and hierarchy paths, spanning multiple documents as needed.
+5. **Scope limiting:** Configurable traversal depth limits (default: 2 hops). Edge type filtering based on query type.
-**Critical property:** This step is **document-agnostic** — the graph is unified, so traversal naturally crosses document boundaries.
+**Output:** A candidate set of requirement/test case nodes with their associated standards sections and hierarchy paths, potentially spanning multiple documents, MNOs, and releases.
-### 7.3 Targeted Vector RAG
+### 7.4 Targeted Vector RAG
-**Objective:** Rank and select the most relevant chunks from the candidate set identified by graph scoping.
+**Objective:** Rank and select the most relevant chunks from the candidate set.
**Process:**
1. Embed the user's query
-2. Compute similarity **only against chunks in the candidate set** (not the entire corpus)
-3. Select top-K chunks, enforcing diversity across documents (e.g., at least 1 chunk from each contributing document if multiple are relevant)
+2. Compute similarity **only against chunks in the candidate set** (using metadata filters for `mno`, `release`, `doc_type` as needed)
+3. Select top-K chunks, enforcing diversity:
+   - Across documents (at least 1 chunk from each contributing document)
+   - Across MNOs (for comparison queries — ensure balanced representation from each MNO)
+   - Across doc types (if both requirements and test cases are relevant)

-**Why targeted, not global:** Global vector search over the entire corpus is what pure RAG does — and it fails here. By restricting to the graph-scoped candidate set, we ensure cross-document coverage (the graph already identified the relevant documents) while using vector similarity for fine-grained ranking within that set.
+### 7.5 Context Assembly

-**Top-K selection with document diversity:** If the candidate set spans 3 documents, a simple top-K by similarity might return all chunks from one document. Apply a diversity constraint: allocate K proportionally across contributing documents (or at minimum, include the highest-ranked chunk from each contributing document).
+**Objective:** Build the LLM prompt context from selected chunks, augmented with structural and relational information from the graph, and clearly annotated with MNO/release provenance.

+**For version diff queries:**

+| Test Cases | If available for PoC docs | All test case docs |
+| Standards | Referenced 3GPP sections from PoC docs only | Full set of referenced standards, multiple releases |

-| **6** | Knowledge graph construction | Is the graph structure correct and complete? | NetworkX graph, visualizable, queryable |

+| **1** | Set up folder structure; PDF text extraction for all 5 VZW docs | Clean structured text from VZW PDFs? | Raw text + extracted tables per document |
+| **2** | Build VZW structural parser for requirements | Reliable extraction of IDs, hierarchy, section text? | Requirement tree JSON per document |

### 9.4 PoC Evaluation
| Category | Example | Count | What It Tests |

**Evaluation criteria:**
| **Completeness** | Did the answer include information from ALL relevant documents? | > 80% of cross-doc questions |
| **Accuracy** | Are cited requirement IDs correct? Are factual claims supported by the source text? | > 90% |
-| **Citation quality** | Are specific VZ_REQ_* IDs cited for each claim? | 100% of answers include citations |
+| **Citation quality** | Are specific requirement/test case IDs cited for each claim? | 100% of answers include citations |
| **Standards integration** | When the answer depends on 3GPP text, is it correctly incorporated? | > 80% of standards questions |
| **No hallucination** | Does the system avoid fabricating requirements or misrepresenting text? | 0 hallucinated requirements |
2. Graph scoping demonstrably improves retrieval over pure vector RAG (A/B comparison on the same test questions)
3. Standards integration enables the system to provide complete answers for requirements that defer to 3GPP
4. The architecture is demonstrably portable — no Claude/Gemini-specific features that can't be replicated with the proprietary LLM
+5. The graph and vector store metadata schema supports future expansion to multi-MNO and multi-release without structural changes

| 1 | **PDF extraction quality** — tables, diagrams, and complex formatting may not extract cleanly | High | Medium | Evaluate multiple extraction libraries (pymupdf, pdfplumber, unstructured.io). Fall back to manual correction for PoC. |

Measure edge recall during PoC. Add missing edges from evaluation failures. |

+| 4 | **Graph traversal explosion** — traversing too many edges returns an unmanageably large candidate set, especially in unified graph with multiple MNOs | Medium | Medium | Configurable depth limits (default: 2 hops). Edge type filtering based on query type. MNO/release metadata filtering at every traversal step. |

+| 5 | **Embedding quality for telecom terminology** — standard models may not handle 3GPP acronyms and terminology well | Medium | Medium | Contextualized chunks (hierarchy path + MNO/release prepended) mitigate partially. Evaluate retrieval quality. If insufficient, explore domain-adapted embedding models. |

+| 6 | **Standards section availability and versioning** — obtaining the correct version/release of referenced 3GPP sections may be complex; different MNOs reference different releases | Medium | Medium | 3GPP specs are publicly available at 3gpp.org. | 7 | **LLM portability** — patterns that work with Claude/Gemini may not transfer to the proprietary LLM | Medium | Medium | Avoid Claude/Gemini-specific features (tool use, system prompts). Use simple, well-documented prompt patterns. Test prompt portability early. |

-| 8 | **Scale at production** — NetworkX and FAISS may not handle GBs of documents across multiple MNOs and versions | Low (PoC) / High (Prod) | High | PoC uses lightweight tools. Production design will specify Neo4j + production vector DB. Scale testing is a separate phase. |

+| 8 | **Unified graph scale** — single graph spanning all MNOs × releases could become very large | Low (PoC) / High (Prod) | High | PoC validates architecture with single MNO+release. Production uses Neo4j with indexing on `mno` + `release` attributes. Monitor query latency as graph grows. Physical partitioning (sharding by MNO) is a fallback if needed. |

| 9 | **Relationship type classification errors** — LLM may misclassify defer/constrain/override relationships with standards | Medium | Medium | Include representative examples in classification prompts. Validate classification on a sample during PoC. |

-| 10 | **Version diff complexity** — structural changes across versions (renumbered sections, reorganized requirements) may break simple ID-based matching | Medium | Medium | Match by requirement ID first (stable across versions). Fall back to text similarity for reorganized sections. |

Fall back to section number + text similarity for reorganized sections. Flag low-confidence matches for human review. |

Version the parsers alongside MNO releases. Detect format changes early by validating extraction output structure. |

For ambiguous MNO, ask for clarification or search across all (with clear attribution in response). Log resolution decisions for transparency. |

# Technical Design Document: Telecom Requirements AI System

**Version:** 0.1 (Draft)
**Date:** April 2026
**Status:** PoC Design Phase

---

## Table of Contents

1. [Executive Summary](#1-executive-summary)
2. [Problem Statement](#2-problem-statement)
3. [System Constraints](#3-system-constraints)
4. [Architecture Overview](#4-architecture-overview)
5. [Ingestion Pipeline](#5-ingestion-pipeline)
   - 5.1 PDF Text Extraction
   - 5.2 Structural Parser
   - 5.3 Cross-Reference Extraction
   - 5.4 Standards Specs Ingestion
   - 5.5 Feature Taxonomy Derivation
   - 5.6 Knowledge Graph Construction
   - 5.7 Vector Store Construction
6. [Knowledge Graph Model](#6-knowledge-graph-model)
   - 6.1 Node Types
   - 6.2 Edge Types
   - 6.3 Cross-Document Handling
7. [Query Pipeline](#7-query-pipeline)
   - 7.1 Query Analysis
   - 7.2 Graph Scoping
   - 7.3 Targeted Vector RAG
   - 7.4 Context Assembly
   - 7.5 LLM Synthesis
8. [Target Capabilities](#8-target-capabilities)
   - 8.1 Requirement Q&A Bot
   - 8.2 Requirement Compliance Agent
9. [PoC Plan](#9-poc-plan)
10. [Risks and Mitigations](#10-risks-and-mitigations)

---

## 1. Executive Summary

This document defines the technical design for an AI system that enables intelligent querying, cross-referencing, and compliance analysis of US Mobile Network Operator (MNO) device requirement specifications. The system combines a **Knowledge Graph** with **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** to overcome the limitations of pure vector-based RAG, which fails to capture the deep inter-dependencies between requirements across documents and referenced telecom standards.

The core architectural insight is: **use a Knowledge Graph to determine WHERE to look, use vector RAG to determine WHAT is most relevant within that scope, and use the requirement hierarchy to provide structural CONTEXT for LLM synthesis.**

The system will be built incrementally — starting with a Proof of Concept (PoC) on a small set of publicly available MNO requirements using commercial LLMs (Claude/Gemini), then scaling to a production deployment using a proprietary LLM and on-premise infrastructure.

---

## 2. Problem Statement

### 2.1 Background

US Mobile Network Operators (e.g., Verizon, AT&T, T-Mobile) release device requirement specifications quarterly. These documents:

- Are based on telecom standards (3GPP, GSMA, OMA) with MNO-specific customizations
- Cover functional areas such as LTE data retry, SMS, OTA device management, activation, IMS, etc.
- Contain Android device preload requirements, platform customizations, and MNO service integration specifications
- Include tables, diagrams, and attachments
- Are accompanied by corresponding test case documents
- Follow a consistent internal structure per MNO (e.g., all Verizon OA docs share the same format)
- Total several hundred megabytes per quarterly release

### 2.2 Key Challenges

**Cross-document dependencies:** A single logical capability (e.g., device activation) may be defined across multiple requirement documents (SIM, UI, Network, Entitlement). No single document contains the complete picture.

**Requirement inter-dependencies:** Requirements in one document may be functionally dependent on requirements in another document (e.g., Data Retry behavior changes based on whether the device supports SMS over IMS, which is defined in the SMS document).

**Standards layering:** MNO requirements reference specific sections of 3GPP/GSMA standards, with three relationship types — defer to the standard, constrain an optional behavior, or override the default. Many MNO requirements are incomplete without the referenced standards text.

**Version management:** Requirements change quarterly. Tracking deltas between versions is a core use case.

### 2.3 Why Pure RAG Fails

Pure vector-based RAG was evaluated and found inadequate because:

1. **No relationship awareness:** Vector retrieval treats each chunk independently. It cannot follow "this requirement depends on that one in another document."
2. **Undirected retrieval scope:** Semantic search over the entire corpus may return chunks from one document while missing critical related chunks in another.
3. **Destroyed structure:** Chunking destroys the hierarchical parent-child relationships between requirements that carry essential context.
4. **Poor telecom terminology handling:** Standard embedding models were not trained on 3GPP terminology, leading to poor retrieval for acronym-heavy queries.
5. **Missing standards context:** Requirements that say "follow 3GPP TS 24.301 section 5.5.1" are incomplete without the referenced standards text, which pure RAG does not incorporate.

---

## 3. System Constraints

| Constraint | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **LLM** | Proprietary foundational LLM for production; supports thinking mode; context window size TBD (potentially up to 2M tokens) |
| **Infrastructure** | On-premise deployment; no external LLM or cloud AI services (data privacy/security) |
| **Open source** | Some flexibility to use open-source models on-premise |
| **PoC LLM** | Claude or Gemini (using only publicly available MNO requirements) |
| **PoC environment** | Personal PC |
| **Data sensitivity** | Production data is proprietary MNO requirements; PoC uses only publicly available documents |
| **Scale (production)** | Hundreds of documents across multiple MNOs and quarterly releases; total corpus can be several GBs |
| **Scale (PoC)** | 5 Verizon OA documents (Feb 2026 release) |

---

## 4. Architecture Overview

The system consists of two major pipelines: **Ingestion** (offline, document processing) and **Query** (online, user-facing).

### 4.1 High-Level Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                        INGESTION PIPELINE                           │
│                                                                     │
│  ┌─────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌─────────────────────┐        │
│  │  MNO    │    │  Structural  │    │  Cross-Reference    │        │
│  │  PDFs   │───>│  Parser      │───>│  Extraction         │        │
│  └─────────┘    │  (per-MNO    │    │  (regex + LLM)      │        │
│                 │   format)    │    └────────┬────────────┘        │
│  ┌─────────┐    └──────┬───────┘             │                     │
│  │  3GPP   │           │              ┌──────┴──────┐              │
│  │  Specs  │──┐        │              │  Feature    │              │
│  └─────────┘  │   ┌────┴────┐         │  Taxonomy   │              │
│               │   │Req Tree │         │  Derivation │              │
│               │   │  JSON   │         │  (LLM)      │              │
│               │   └────┬────┘         └──────┬──────┘              │
│               │        │                     │                     │
│               │        ▼                     ▼                     │
│               │   ┌─────────────────────────────────┐              │
│               └──>│       KNOWLEDGE GRAPH            │              │
│                   │  Requirement nodes + hierarchy   │              │
│                   │  Cross-ref edges                 │              │
│                   │  Feature nodes + mappings        │              │
│                   │  Standards nodes + typed edges   │              │
│                   └─────────────────────────────────┘              │
│                                    │                               │
│                   ┌────────────────┴────────────────┐              │
│                   │         VECTOR STORE             │              │
│                   │  Contextualized requirement      │              │
│                   │  chunks with hierarchy metadata  │              │
│                   └─────────────────────────────────┘              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                         QUERY PIPELINE                              │
│                                                                     │
│  User Query                                                         │
│      │                                                              │
│      ▼                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────────┐                                               │
│  │  Query Analysis  │  LLM extracts entities, features, intent      │
│  └────────┬─────────┘                                               │
│           ▼                                                         │
│  ┌──────────────────┐                                               │
│  │  Graph Scoping   │  Entity lookup + feature lookup +             │
│  │                  │  edge traversal across documents               │
│  │                  │  → candidate requirement set                   │
│  └────────┬─────────┘                                               │
│           ▼                                                         │
│  ┌──────────────────┐                                               │
│  │  Targeted RAG    │  Vector similarity WITHIN candidate set       │
│  │                  │  → ranked, selected top-K chunks               │
│  └────────┬─────────┘                                               │
│           ▼                                                         │
│  ┌──────────────────┐                                               │
│  │ Context Assembly │  Selected chunks + hierarchy paths +          │
│  │                  │  cross-ref context + standards text            │
│  └────────┬─────────┘                                               │
│           ▼                                                         │
│  ┌──────────────────┐                                               │
│  │  LLM Synthesis   │  Answer with citations (VZ_REQ_* IDs)        │
│  └──────────────────┘                                               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

---

## 5. Ingestion Pipeline

### 5.1 PDF Text Extraction

**Objective:** Extract raw text, tables, and structural markers from MNO requirement PDFs.

**Tooling:** `pymupdf` (PyMuPDF) or `pdfplumber` for text and table extraction.

**Output:** Raw text per page with preserved layout information, plus separately extracted tables.

**Key considerations:**
- Tables are a major information carrier (compliance matrices, parameter tables, cause code mappings). Table extraction must preserve row/column structure.
- Diagrams and flow charts will be extracted as images for potential future multimodal processing. For PoC, diagram content will be handled via manual annotation if needed.
- Headers, footers, and page numbers must be stripped to avoid noise.
- The extraction must preserve enough layout information for the structural parser to identify section hierarchy.

### 5.2 Structural Parser

**Objective:** Parse extracted text into a structured requirement tree, leveraging the consistent per-MNO document format.

**Approach:** Rule-based parser specific to each MNO's document format. For VZW OA documents, the parser exploits:

- **Requirement ID pattern:** `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}` — globally unique, with plan name embedded
- **Section numbering:** Hierarchical numbering (e.g., `1.4.3.1.1.10`) up to 6+ levels deep
- **Plan metadata:** Plan Name, Plan ID, Version Number, Release Date in document header
- **Consistent section zones:**
  - Sections 1.1–1.2: Metadata (applicability, acronyms, hardware specs)
  - Section 1.3: Core software specifications (timers, algorithms, rules)
  - Section 1.4: Scenario-based requirements

**Output per document:** Structured JSON:

```json
{
  "plan_id": "LTEDATARETRY",
  "plan_name": "LTE_Data_Retry",
  "version": 39,
  "release_date": "February 2026",
  "requirements": [
    {
      "req_id": "VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7748",
      "section_number": "1.4.3.1.1.2",
      "title": "EMM Cause Codes 3, 6, 7, 8",
      "parent_req_id": "VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7747",
      "parent_section": "1.4.3.1.1",
      "hierarchy_path": [
        "LTE Data Retry",
        "SCENARIOS",
        "EMM SPECIFIC PROCEDURES",
        "ATTACH REQUEST",
        "ATTACH REJECT WITH EMM CAUSE CODE",
        "EMM Cause Codes 3, 6, 7, 8"
      ],
      "text": "...",
      "tables": [...],
      "children": ["VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_..."],
      "cross_references": {
        "internal": ["VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_2376"],
        "external_plans": ["LTESMS"],
        "standards": [
          {
            "spec": "3GPP TS 24.301",
            "section": "5.5.1.2.5",
            "release": "Release 10"
          }
        ]
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

### 5.3 Cross-Reference Extraction

**Objective:** Identify all references from each requirement to other requirements, other VZW plans, and external standards.

**Three extraction methods, in order of reliability:**

**Method 1: Explicit Plan References (Deterministic)**
- Pattern match for `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}` IDs referencing other plans
- Pattern match for known plan names (e.g., "LTE_SMS", "LTE_Data_Retry") in requirement text
- Reliability: High

**Method 2: Standards References (Deterministic)**
- Regex extraction of 3GPP spec citations: `3GPP TS \d+\.\d+`, section numbers, release versions
- Regex for GSMA/OMA spec citations
- Reliability: High

**Method 3: Concept/Feature References (LLM-Assisted)**
- For each requirement, LLM extracts telecom concept tags (timers, procedures, interfaces, cause codes, device modes, protocols)
- Requirements across documents sharing concept tags are candidate cross-document links
- Reliability: Medium — requires validation

**Output:** A cross-reference manifest per document listing all outbound references with their types.

### 5.4 Standards Specs Ingestion (Hybrid Selective — Option C)

**Objective:** Ingest the specific sections of 3GPP/GSMA standards that VZW requirements reference, with enough surrounding context for completeness.

**Approach:**

**Step 1: Collect all standards references across all ingested VZW documents**
```
Aggregate output:
- 3GPP TS 24.301 sections: 5.5.1.2.5, 5.3.7a, 5.4.2, ... (Release 10)
- 3GPP TS 24.008 sections: ...
- GSMA PRD IR.92 sections: ...
- etc.
```

**Step 2: For each referenced section, extract from the standards document:**
- The referenced section itself (primary content)
- The parent section (structural context)
- Definitions/terminology sections referenced within the section
- Adjacent sub-sections that are part of the same procedure

This produces a **focused but contextually complete** subset of each standard — estimated at 5–10% of the full spec per standard.

**Step 3: Classify the relationship between each VZW requirement and its referenced standards section:**

Using the LLM during ingestion:

| Relationship | Meaning | Detection Signal |
|-------------|---------|-----------------|
| **DEFER** | VZW says "follow 3GPP" with no modifications | "shall follow the steps detailed in 3GPP...", "per 3GPP TS..." with no additional constraints |
| **CONSTRAIN** | VZW narrows an optional behavior or makes it mandatory | "shall implement X on a per-PLMN basis" where 3GPP leaves this as implementation choice |
| **OVERRIDE** | VZW specifies behavior that differs from 3GPP default | Explicit timer values, specific parameter settings that differ from 3GPP defaults |
| **EXTEND** | VZW adds requirements beyond what 3GPP specifies | Requirements with no corresponding 3GPP section, VZW-specific scenarios |

**Step 4: Store the delta summary as edge metadata**
The LLM produces a concise summary of what specifically differs (e.g., "VZW mandates T3402 on per-PLMN basis; 3GPP leaves as implementation choice"). This is pre-computed so that "how does VZW differ from 3GPP?" queries can be answered without re-analysis at query time.

### 5.5 Feature Taxonomy Derivation

**Objective:** Build a feature/capability taxonomy bottom-up from the documents themselves, enabling cross-document grouping of requirements by logical feature.

**This addresses Pattern (a) of cross-document requirements** — where a single logical capability is defined across multiple documents without explicit cross-references.

**Approach: Two-step LLM-driven derivation**

**Step 1: Document-Level Feature Extraction**

For each document, feed plan metadata + section headings (TOC) to the LLM:

```
Prompt:
You are a telecom domain expert. Given the following VZW requirement
document table of contents, extract:
1. The primary telecom features/capabilities this document covers
2. Other features/capabilities this document DEPENDS ON or REFERENCES
   (even if primarily defined elsewhere)
3. Key telecom concepts, protocols, and interfaces mentioned

Output as structured JSON.
```

**Step 2: Cross-Document Consolidation**

Feed all per-document feature extractions to the LLM:

```
Prompt:
Given feature extractions from these VZW requirement documents, identify:
1. Features that appear in MULTIPLE documents (cross-cutting features)
2. Dependency chains between documents
3. A consolidated feature taxonomy

Output a feature taxonomy with:
- Feature name and description
- Contributing documents
- Dependency relationships between features
```

**Step 3: Human Review**

The derived taxonomy is reviewed by a domain expert to:
- Correct incorrectly merged or split features
- Validate dependency directions
- Add features the LLM missed

**Output:** A feature taxonomy:

```json
{
  "features": [
    {
      "feature_id": "DEVICE_ACTIVATION",
      "name": "Device Activation",
      "description": "End-to-end device activation flow including SIM provisioning, UI flow, initial network attach, and entitlement checks",
      "contributing_plans": ["LTESIM", "LTEUI", "LTENETWORK", "LTEENTITLEMENT", "LTEDATARETRY"],
      "depends_on_features": ["IMS_REGISTRATION", "PDN_CONNECTIVITY"],
      "keywords": ["activation", "initial attach", "provisioning", "first boot"]
    }
  ]
}
```

### 5.6 Knowledge Graph Construction

**Objective:** Build a unified knowledge graph across all ingested documents, standards, and features.

See [Section 6: Knowledge Graph Model](#6-knowledge-graph-model) for the full graph schema.

**Tooling:** NetworkX (PoC) or Neo4j (production).

**Construction sequence:**
1. Create requirement nodes from structural parser output (per document)
2. Create hierarchy edges (parent-child) within each document
3. Create cross-reference edges (from cross-reference extraction)
4. Create standards nodes (from standards ingestion)
5. Create standards edges with relationship types and delta metadata
6. Create feature nodes (from derived taxonomy)
7. Create feature mapping edges (requirement → feature)

### 5.7 Vector Store Construction

**Objective:** Create embeddings for requirement nodes for use in targeted vector retrieval.

**Chunk strategy:** Each requirement node becomes one chunk. This is a critical design choice — we do NOT use arbitrary fixed-size chunking. The structural parser has already produced semantically meaningful units (individual requirements).

**Contextualization:** Before embedding, each chunk is prepended with its structural context:

```
[Plan: LTE_Data_Retry | Version: 39 | Feb 2026]
[Path: SCENARIOS > EMM SPECIFIC PROCEDURES > ATTACH REQUEST > ATTACH REJECT WITH EMM CAUSE CODE > EMM Cause Codes 3, 6, 7, 8]
[Req ID: VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7748]

<requirement text>
```

This follows the principle from Anthropic's Contextual Retrieval research — adding document-level and structural context to each chunk before embedding improves retrieval accuracy.

**Tooling:** FAISS or ChromaDB (PoC); production vector store TBD based on infrastructure.

**Embedding model considerations:**
- Standard embedding models (e.g., `text-embedding-3-large`) may underperform on telecom terminology
- If retrieval quality is insufficient, consider fine-tuning an embedding model on telecom text or using a domain-adapted model
- This is an optimization to evaluate during PoC — start with a general-purpose model and measure

---

## 6. Knowledge Graph Model

### 6.1 Node Types

| Node Type | Description | Key Attributes | Source |
|-----------|-------------|---------------|--------|
| **Requirement** | An individual requirement from an MNO document | `req_id`, `plan_id`, `section_number`, `title`, `text`, `tables`, `hierarchy_path` | Structural parser |
| **Plan** | An MNO requirement document | `plan_id`, `plan_name`, `version`, `release_date`, `mno` | Document metadata |
| **Standard_Section** | A specific section of a 3GPP/GSMA/OMA standard | `spec_id` (e.g., "3GPP_TS_24.301"), `section`, `release`, `text` | Standards ingestion |
| **Feature** | A logical telecom capability spanning multiple documents | `feature_id`, `name`, `description`, `keywords` | Taxonomy derivation |
| **Test_Case** | A test case from a test plan document | `test_id`, `plan_id`, `description`, `steps`, `expected_result` | Test plan parser |

### 6.2 Edge Types

#### Within-Document Edges

| Edge Type | From → To | Meaning |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| `parent_of` | Requirement → Requirement | Hierarchical containment (section 1.4.3 contains 1.4.3.1) |
| `belongs_to` | Requirement → Plan | Requirement is part of this plan document |

#### Cross-Document Edges

| Edge Type | From → To | Meaning | Detection |
|-----------|-----------|---------|-----------|
| `depends_on` | Requirement → Requirement (other plan) | Functional dependency — behavior changes based on the other requirement | Explicit plan references in text (deterministic) |
| `shared_standard` | Requirement ↔ Requirement | Both reference the same standards section | Matching 3GPP section references (deterministic) |
| `concept_link` | Requirement ↔ Requirement | Related via shared telecom concept | LLM-extracted concept tags (medium confidence) |

#### Standards Edges

| Edge Type | From → To | Meaning | Attributes |
|-----------|-----------|---------|------------|
| `defers_to` | Requirement → Standard_Section | "Do what 3GPP says" — actual behavior is in the standard | `delta_summary`: null |
| `constrains` | Requirement → Standard_Section | Narrows an optional behavior | `delta_summary`: what is constrained |
| `overrides` | Requirement → Standard_Section | Differs from standard default | `delta_summary`: what differs and how |
| `extends` | Requirement → Standard_Section | Adds beyond what standard specifies | `delta_summary`: what is added |
| `parent_section` | Standard_Section → Standard_Section | Structural hierarchy within a standard | — |

#### Feature Edges

| Edge Type | From → To | Meaning |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| `maps_to` | Requirement → Feature | This requirement contributes to this feature/capability |
| `feature_depends_on` | Feature → Feature | One feature requires another |

#### Traceability Edges

| Edge Type | From → To | Meaning |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| `tested_by` | Requirement → Test_Case | This test case validates this requirement |
| `tests` | Test_Case → Requirement | Inverse of above |

### 6.3 Cross-Document Handling

The graph handles two distinct cross-document patterns:

#### Pattern (a): Fragmented Requirements — One Capability Across Multiple Documents

A single logical capability (e.g., device activation) is defined across multiple documents. No single document has the complete picture, and the documents may not explicitly cross-reference each other.

**Handled via Feature Nodes.** The feature taxonomy groups requirements from different documents under a common feature. A query about "device activation requirements" resolves to the `DEVICE_ACTIVATION` feature node, which maps to requirements across SIM, UI, Network, Entitlement, and Data Retry documents.

```
              ┌─────────────────────┐
              │  DEVICE_ACTIVATION  │  ← Feature Node
              └──────────┬──────────┘
     ┌──────────┬────────┼────────┬──────────┐
     ▼          ▼        ▼        ▼          ▼
 [SIM reqs] [UI reqs] [Network] [Entitle.] [DataRetry]
```

#### Pattern (b): Dependent Requirements — Behavior Depends on Another Document

A requirement in one document is functionally incomplete without understanding a requirement in another document.

**Handled via typed cross-document edges.** The `depends_on`, `conditional_on` edges directly link dependent requirements. Graph traversal follows these edges at query time.

```
VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_41013 ──depends_on──> VZ_REQ_LTESMS_XXXX
   (Attach Reject behavior)                (SMS over IMS support)
```

---

## 7. Query Pipeline

### 7.1 Query Analysis

**Input:** User's natural language query.

**Process:** The LLM analyzes the query to extract:

| Extracted Element | Example | Purpose |
|-------------------|---------|---------|
| **Named entities** | Requirement IDs, plan names, timer names, cause codes | Direct graph lookup |
| **Telecom concepts** | "Attach Reject", "IMS registration", "VoWiFi" | Feature and concept matching |
| **Referenced standards** | "3GPP TS 24.301" | Standards node lookup |
| **Query type** | Single-doc Q&A, cross-doc Q&A, standards comparison, traceability, version diff | Determines pipeline behavior |
| **Scope indicators** | Specific document names, version numbers, functional areas | Narrows graph traversal |

**Output:** A structured query intent object.

```json
{
  "entities": ["EMM Cause Code 19", "SMS over IMS"],
  "concepts": ["attach_reject", "sms_over_ims"],
  "likely_plans": ["LTEDATARETRY", "LTESMS"],
  "standards_refs": ["3GPP TS 24.301"],
  "query_type": "cross_doc_qa",
  "scope": null
}
```

### 7.2 Graph Scoping

**Objective:** Use the knowledge graph to identify a candidate set of requirement nodes that are potentially relevant, spanning multiple documents as needed.

**Process:**

1. **Entity lookup:** Match extracted entities directly to graph nodes (requirement IDs, plan names, timer names, etc.)

2. **Feature lookup:** Match extracted concepts to feature nodes, then follow `maps_to` edges to find all contributing requirements

3. **Edge traversal:** From the initial nodes found in steps 1–2, traverse edges:
   - `depends_on` → pull in dependent requirements from other documents
   - `defers_to` / `constrains` / `overrides` → pull in referenced standards sections
   - `parent_of` (upward) → include parent context for structural understanding
   - `shared_standard` → find requirements in other docs referencing the same standard section
   - `tested_by` → include test cases if the query is traceability-related

4. **Scope limiting:** Apply configurable traversal depth limits to prevent explosion. Default: 2 hops from initial nodes.

**Output:** A candidate set of requirement nodes (typically 20–100) with their associated standards sections and hierarchy paths, spanning multiple documents as needed.

**Critical property:** This step is **document-agnostic** — the graph is unified, so traversal naturally crosses document boundaries.

### 7.3 Targeted Vector RAG

**Objective:** Rank and select the most relevant chunks from the candidate set identified by graph scoping.

**Process:**
1. Embed the user's query
2. Compute similarity **only against chunks in the candidate set** (not the entire corpus)
3. Select top-K chunks, enforcing diversity across documents (e.g., at least 1 chunk from each contributing document if multiple are relevant)

**Why targeted, not global:** Global vector search over the entire corpus is what pure RAG does — and it fails here. By restricting to the graph-scoped candidate set, we ensure cross-document coverage (the graph already identified the relevant documents) while using vector similarity for fine-grained ranking within that set.

**Top-K selection with document diversity:** If the candidate set spans 3 documents, a simple top-K by similarity might return all chunks from one document. Apply a diversity constraint: allocate K proportionally across contributing documents (or at minimum, include the highest-ranked chunk from each contributing document).

### 7.4 Context Assembly

**Objective:** Build the LLM prompt context from selected chunks, augmented with structural and relational information from the graph.

**For each selected requirement chunk, include:**

1. **The requirement text** (from the chunk)
2. **Hierarchy path** — the full path from root to this requirement (e.g., `SCENARIOS > EMM SPECIFIC PROCEDURES > ATTACH REQUEST > ATTACH REJECT > Cause Code 19`)
3. **Parent context** — if the parent section provides essential framing, include a summary
4. **Standards text** — if the requirement has a `defers_to` edge, include the referenced standards section text. If `constrains` or `overrides`, include the delta summary.
5. **Cross-reference annotations** — note which other requirements in the context are related and how (e.g., "This requirement depends on VZ_REQ_LTESMS_XXXX, also included below")

**Context template:**

```
You are answering a question about MNO device requirements.
The following requirements are relevant, drawn from multiple documents.

=== REQUIREMENT 1 ===
Document: LTE_Data_Retry (v39, Feb 2026)
Hierarchy: SCENARIOS > EMM SPECIFIC PROCEDURES > ATTACH REQUEST > ...
Req ID: VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7751
Relationship: This requirement depends on VZ_REQ_LTESMS_XXXX (see Req 3)
Standards Reference: Defers to 3GPP TS 24.301 section 5.5.1.2.5

<requirement text>

=== REQUIREMENT 2 ===
...

=== REFERENCED STANDARD ===
Source: 3GPP TS 24.301, Release 10, Section 5.5.1.2.5
Relationship to VZ_REQ_LTEDATARETRY_7751: DEFER (VZW adopts 3GPP behavior as-is)

<standards section text>

=== REQUIREMENT 3 (from different document) ===
Document: LTE_SMS (v42, Feb 2026)
...
```

### 7.5 LLM Synthesis

**Objective:** Generate a comprehensive, accurate answer with proper citations.

**The LLM is instructed to:**
1. Synthesize information across all provided requirements and standards references
2. Cite specific requirement IDs (`VZ_REQ_*`) for every factual claim
3. Note when requirements from different documents interact or depend on each other
4. Distinguish between MNO-specific requirements and standard 3GPP behavior
5. Flag if the provided context appears insufficient to fully answer the query

**For standards comparison queries** ("How does VZW differ from 3GPP for X?"):
- The LLM receives both the VZW requirement text and the 3GPP standards text
- The pre-computed delta summary (from ingestion) is included as a hint
- The LLM produces a structured comparison

---

## 8. Target Capabilities

### 8.1 Requirement Q&A Bot

#### 8.1.1 Single-Document Q&A

**Query example:** "What timer values are specified for T3402 in the Data Retry requirements?"

**Pipeline:** Query analysis → entity lookup (T3402, LTEDATARETRY) → graph scoping (single document) → RAG ranking → context assembly → synthesis.

#### 8.1.2 Cross-Document Q&A

**Query example:** "What are the full requirements for handling Attach Reject when the device doesn't support SMS over IMS?"

**Pipeline:** Query analysis → entity lookup (Attach Reject, SMS over IMS) → graph scoping (traverses from LTEDATARETRY to LTESMS via `depends_on` edge) → RAG ranking across both docs → context assembly with cross-reference annotations → synthesis.

#### 8.1.3 Standards Comparison

**Query example:** "How does VZW's T3402 handling differ from the 3GPP specification?"

**Pipeline:** Query analysis → entity lookup (T3402) → graph scoping (VZW requirement + standards nodes via `constrains`/`overrides` edge) → context assembly with both VZW and 3GPP text + pre-computed delta summary → synthesis as structured comparison.

#### 8.1.4 Version Diff

**Query example:** "What changed in the Data Retry requirements between 2025Q4 and 2026Q1?"

**Pipeline:** Both versions of the document are ingested as separate plan nodes in the graph (same `plan_id`, different `version`). The query triggers a diff operation:
1. Identify matching requirements across versions (by `req_id`)
2. Detect added, removed, and modified requirements
3. Present a structured diff with context

**Note:** This requires version-aware graph nodes — each ingested plan version is a distinct subgraph.

#### 8.1.5 Traceability

**Query example:** "What test cases cover the Attach Reject requirements?"

**Pipeline:** Query analysis → entity lookup → graph scoping (follow `tested_by` edges to test case nodes) → context assembly → synthesis listing requirements mapped to test cases.

### 8.2 Requirement Compliance Agent

**Note:** The compliance agent is a more complex, agentic workflow. It is scoped for post-PoC development but the architecture is outlined here for completeness.

#### 8.2.1 Single Requirement Compliance Check

**Input:** A compliance sheet (Excel) containing requirement IDs, compliance status, and R&D comments.

**Process:**
1. Parse the compliance sheet into structured records
2. For each requirement marked as "compliant" or with R&D comments:
   - Look up the requirement in the knowledge graph
   - Retrieve the full requirement text with context
   - Evaluate whether the R&D comment accurately reflects the requirement
   - Flag discrepancies (e.g., comment describes a behavior that contradicts the requirement text)

#### 8.2.2 Cross-Document Compliance Consistency

**Input:** Compliance sheets for multiple requirement documents.

**Process:**
1. Identify dependent requirement groups using the knowledge graph (e.g., Data Retry requirements that depend on SMS support)
2. For each dependency chain, check consistency:
   - If Requirement A in Doc 1 is marked "not supported", and Requirement B in Doc 2 depends on A and is marked "supported" — flag inconsistency
   - Use the typed dependency edges (`depends_on`, `conditional_on`) to determine which consistency checks to apply

#### 8.2.3 Auto-Fill from Module Documentation

**Input:** Compliance sheet + module/chipset documentation (e.g., Qualcomm modem spec).

**Process:**
1. For requirements tagged as modem-layer (identified via the `SOFTWARE ARCHITECTURE WITHIN THE UE` section in VZW docs)
2. Match requirement content against module documentation
3. Pre-fill compliance status with evidence citations

#### 8.2.4 Delta Compliance Sheet Generation

**Input:** Base compliance sheets (previous version) + delta compliance information (changes for new version).

**Process:**
1. Use version diff (Section 8.1.4) to identify changed requirements
2. Carry forward compliance for unchanged requirements
3. Apply delta compliance information for changed requirements
4. Flag new requirements that need manual review

---

## 9. PoC Plan

### 9.1 Objective

Validate that the Knowledge Graph + RAG architecture can reliably answer cross-document questions that pure RAG cannot, using a small set of publicly available VZW OA requirement documents.

### 9.2 PoC Scope

| Dimension | PoC Scope | Production Scope |
|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| Documents | 5 VZW OA docs (LTEDATARETRY, LTESMS, LTEAT, LTEB13NAC, LTEOTADM) | All MNO docs across MNOs and quarterly releases |
| Standards | Referenced 3GPP sections from PoC docs only | Full set of referenced standards |
| Features | Derived taxonomy from 5 docs | Comprehensive taxonomy, human-curated |
| Capabilities | Q&A bot only (single-doc, cross-doc, standards comparison) | Q&A bot + compliance agent + version diff |
| LLM | Claude or Gemini | Proprietary LLM |
| Infrastructure | Local Python environment | On-premise servers |
| Vector store | FAISS or ChromaDB | Production vector DB |
| Graph store | NetworkX (in-memory) | Neo4j or similar |

### 9.3 PoC Steps

| Step | Description | Validates | Deliverable |
|------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| **1** | PDF text extraction for all 5 VZW docs | Can we get clean, structured text from VZW PDFs? | Raw text + extracted tables per document |
| **2** | Build structural parser for VZW OA format | Can we reliably extract requirement IDs, hierarchy, section text? | Requirement tree JSON per document |
| **3** | Cross-reference extraction | Can we identify inter-document and standards references? | Cross-reference manifest per document |
| **4** | LLM-driven feature taxonomy derivation | Does bottom-up taxonomy discovery produce sensible groupings? | Feature taxonomy JSON + human review notes |
| **5** | Selective 3GPP section ingestion | Can we obtain and parse the referenced standards sections? | Standards sections JSON |
| **6** | Knowledge graph construction | Is the graph structure correct and complete? | NetworkX graph, visualizable, queryable |
| **7** | Vector store construction with contextualized chunks | Baseline embedding quality for telecom content | FAISS/ChromaDB index |
| **8** | Query pipeline implementation (graph scoping → targeted RAG → context assembly → LLM synthesis) | **Core validation: can the system answer cross-document questions?** | Working query pipeline |
| **9** | Evaluation on test questions | Quantified performance | Evaluation report |

### 9.4 PoC Evaluation

**Test question set (15–20 questions across categories):**

| Category | Example | Count | What It Tests |
|----------|---------|-------|---------------|
| Single-doc factual | "What is the T3402 timer behavior?" | 3–4 | Baseline: can the system answer from one document? |
| Cross-doc dependency | "How does Data Retry handle devices without SMS over IMS?" | 4–5 | **Core test:** does graph traversal pull from multiple documents? |
| Feature-level | "What are all the requirements related to device activation?" | 3–4 | Does the feature taxonomy enable comprehensive retrieval? |
| Standards comparison | "How does VZW T3402 differ from 3GPP?" | 3–4 | Are standards edges and delta summaries working? |
| Traceability | "What test cases cover EMM cause code handling?" | 2–3 | Do traceability edges work? (if test docs available) |

**Evaluation criteria:**

| Metric | Definition | Target |
|--------|-----------|--------|
| **Completeness** | Did the answer include information from ALL relevant documents? | > 80% of cross-doc questions |
| **Accuracy** | Are cited requirement IDs correct? Are factual claims supported by the source text? | > 90% |
| **Citation quality** | Are specific VZ_REQ_* IDs cited for each claim? | 100% of answers include citations |
| **Standards integration** | When the answer depends on 3GPP text, is it correctly incorporated? | > 80% of standards questions |
| **No hallucination** | Does the system avoid fabricating requirements or misrepresenting text? | 0 hallucinated requirements |

### 9.5 PoC Success Criteria

The PoC is considered successful if:

1. The system correctly answers cross-document questions that require information from 2+ documents, with completeness > 80%
2. Graph scoping demonstrably improves retrieval over pure vector RAG (A/B comparison on the same test questions)
3. Standards integration enables the system to provide complete answers for requirements that defer to 3GPP
4. The architecture is demonstrably portable — no Claude/Gemini-specific features that can't be replicated with the proprietary LLM

---

## 10. Risks and Mitigations

| # | Risk | Severity | Likelihood | Mitigation |
|---|------|----------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | **PDF extraction quality** — tables, diagrams, and complex formatting may not extract cleanly | High | Medium | Evaluate multiple extraction libraries (pymupdf, pdfplumber, unstructured.io). Fall back to manual correction for PoC. |
| 2 | **Cross-document edge completeness** — implicit dependencies between documents may not be detected, leading to incomplete answers | High | Medium | Three-layer extraction strategy (deterministic + standards + LLM-based). Measure edge recall during PoC. Add missing edges from evaluation failures. |
| 3 | **Feature taxonomy quality** — LLM-derived taxonomy may incorrectly group or miss features | Medium | Medium | Human review step is mandatory. Start simple (fewer, broader features) and refine. |
| 4 | **Graph traversal explosion** — traversing too many edges returns an unmanageably large candidate set | Medium | Low | Configurable depth limits (default: 2 hops). Edge type filtering (only follow certain edge types based on query type). |
| 5 | **Embedding quality for telecom terminology** — standard models may not handle 3GPP acronyms and terminology well | Medium | Medium | Contextualized chunks (hierarchy path prepended) mitigate partially. Evaluate retrieval quality. If insufficient, explore domain-adapted embedding models. |
| 6 | **Standards section availability** — obtaining the correct version of referenced 3GPP sections may be complex | Medium | Low | 3GPP specs are publicly available at 3gpp.org. For PoC, manually extract needed sections. For production, build an automated 3GPP spec parser. |
| 7 | **LLM portability** — patterns that work with Claude/Gemini may not transfer to the proprietary LLM | Medium | Medium | Avoid Claude/Gemini-specific features (tool use, system prompts). Use simple, well-documented prompt patterns. Test prompt portability early. |
| 8 | **Scale at production** — NetworkX and FAISS may not handle GBs of documents across multiple MNOs and versions | Low (PoC) / High (Prod) | High | PoC uses lightweight tools. Production design will specify Neo4j + production vector DB. Scale testing is a separate phase. |
| 9 | **Relationship type classification errors** — LLM may misclassify defer/constrain/override relationships with standards | Medium | Medium | Include representative examples in classification prompts. Validate classification on a sample during PoC. |
| 10 | **Version diff complexity** — structural changes across versions (renumbered sections, reorganized requirements) may break simple ID-based matching | Medium | Medium | Match by requirement ID first (stable across versions). Fall back to text similarity for reorganized sections. |

---

## Appendix A: VZW OA Document Structure Reference

Based on analysis of `LTEDATARETRY.pdf` (VZW OA, Feb 2026):

```
Document Format:
  - Header: Plan Name, Plan Id, Version Number, Release Date
  - Requirement ID format: VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}
  - Section numbering: Hierarchical, up to 6+ levels (e.g., 1.4.3.1.1.10)
  - 136 pages

Section Zones:
  1.1    INTRODUCTION (applicability, specs, acronyms, req language)
  1.2    HARDWARE SPECIFICATIONS (mechanical, electrical)
  1.3    SOFTWARE SPECIFICATIONS
    1.3.1  Assumptions (device modes, CSFB, CSG)
    1.3.2  Software Architecture Within the UE
    1.3.3  Generic Throttling Algorithm (rules, details, examples)
    1.3.4–1.3.12  Timers and Specific Behaviors (T3402, T3346, T3245, T3396, etc.)
  1.4    SCENARIOS
    1.4.1  RRC Error scenarios
    1.4.2  EMM Common Procedure scenarios
    1.4.3  EMM Specific Procedure scenarios (Attach, TAU, Service Request)

Cross-Reference Patterns Observed:
  - 3GPP TS 24.301 (NAS protocol) — most frequent
  - References to specific sections and releases (e.g., "Release 10")
  - References to other VZW plan concepts (e.g., "SMS over IMS")
```

---

## Appendix B: Technology Stack (PoC)

| Component | Technology | Notes |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| Language | Python 3.10+ | |
| PDF extraction | pymupdf / pdfplumber | Evaluate both; choose based on table extraction quality |
| Structural parsing | Custom Python (regex + rules) | VZW OA format-specific |
| LLM (PoC) | Claude API or Gemini API | For feature extraction, concept tagging, query synthesis |
| Knowledge graph | NetworkX | In-memory, sufficient for 5 documents |
| Vector store | FAISS or ChromaDB | Local, sufficient for PoC scale |
| Embedding model | text-embedding-3-large (or similar) | Evaluate retrieval quality; consider domain adaptation if needed |
| Orchestration | Python scripts | No framework needed for PoC |

## Appendix C: Technology Considerations (Production)

| Component | Considerations |
|-----------|---------------|
| LLM | Proprietary LLM with thinking mode; context window TBD |
| Knowledge graph | Neo4j or equivalent graph database for scale and query performance |
| Vector store | Production-grade vector DB (Milvus, Weaviate, or similar on-premise option) |
| Embedding model | Evaluate proprietary LLM's embedding capability; otherwise on-premise open-source model (e.g., BGE, E5) |
| Orchestration | Agentic workflow framework for compliance agent; RAG orchestration layer |
| Document pipeline | Automated ingestion pipeline with version management |
| API layer | REST API for Q&A bot and compliance agent interfaces |

content.text: Now let me update the memory to reflect the new design decisions.

content.content:
1	---
2	name: Key Design Decisions and Rationale
3	description: Why we chose specific architectural approaches for the Telecom Requirements AI System — the reasoning behind the TDD
4	type: project
5	---
6	
7	## Decision 1: Knowledge Graph + RAG over Pure RAG
8	
9	**Chose:** Hybrid Knowledge Graph + RAG
10	**Rejected:** Pure vector RAG (already tried, failed)
11	**Why:** User's team already tried pure RAG and it couldn't understand inter-dependencies between requirements. Root cause analysis identified 5 failure modes:
12	1. No relationship awareness — chunks are independent, can't follow cross-doc dependencies
13	2. Undirected retrieval scope — searches entire corpus, may miss critical docs
14	3. Destroyed hierarchy — chunking loses parent-child structure that carries essential context
15	4. Poor telecom terminology handling — standard embeddings don't handle 3GPP acronyms well
16	5. Missing standards context — requirements that say "follow 3GPP TS X" are incomplete without standards text
17	
18	**How to apply:** The graph is the routing layer that fixes problems 1-3. Contextualized chunks help with 4. Standards ingestion fixes 5.
19	
20	## Decision 2: Graph Scoping → Targeted RAG (not Graph OR RAG)
21	
22	**Chose:** Graph narrows scope first, then vector RAG ranks within that scope
23	**Rejected:** Graph-only retrieval, RAG-only retrieval, parallel graph+RAG
24	**Why:**
25	- Graph alone can't handle vague/conceptual queries ("what retry behavior is most aggressive?")
26	- Graph scoping may return too many nodes (30+), RAG helps rank them
27	- Graph may have edge gaps; RAG is the fallback
28	- RAG alone is what already failed
29	- The key insight: "graph tells us WHERE to look, RAG tells us WHAT's most relevant"
30	
31	## Decision 3: Structural Parsing over LLM Extraction
32	
33	**Chose:** Deterministic rule-based parser for document structure, LLM only for enrichment
34	**Rejected:** Pure LLM extraction of requirements and relationships
35	**Why:** VZW OA docs have highly consistent structure (requirement IDs follow `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}`, hierarchical section numbering, consistent zones). This structure is extractable with regex/rules — more reliable and cheaper than LLM extraction. LLM is reserved for what rules can't do: concept tagging, relationship classification, ambiguous cross-references.
36	
37	## Decision 4: Standards Ingestion — Option C (Hybrid Selective)
38	
39	**Chose:** Selectively ingest only referenced 3GPP sections + surrounding context (parent section, definitions, adjacent sub-sections)
40	**Rejected:**
41	- Option A (full ingestion of 3GPP specs) — too massive, TS 24.301 alone is 400+ pages
42	- Option B (only exact referenced sections) — misses context, a section may depend on adjacent definitions
43	**Why:** Option C is tractable for PoC (maybe 50-200 distinct section references across all VZW docs → ~5-10% of each standard), guarantees every ingested section is relevant, and includes enough surrounding context for completeness.
44	
45	## Decision 5: Bottom-Up Feature Taxonomy Derivation
46	
47	**Chose:** Derive taxonomy from documents using LLM, then human review
48	**Rejected:** Pre-defined taxonomy, purely manual taxonomy
49	**Why:** User explicitly chose to derive from documents first rather than imposing a predefined taxonomy. Approach: (1) LLM extracts features per document from TOC/section headings, (2) LLM consolidates across documents, (3) human reviews. This ensures the taxonomy reflects what's actually in the documents.
50	
51	## Decision 6: Two Cross-Document Patterns
52	
53	Identified two distinct patterns that need different graph structures:
54	- **Pattern (a) Fragmented:** One logical capability spans multiple docs (e.g., activation = SIM + UI + Network + Entitlement). No doc cross-references the others. Handled by Feature Nodes that group requirements across docs.

---
name: Key Design Decisions and Rationale
description: Why we chose specific architectural approaches for the Telecom Requirements AI System — the reasoning behind the TDD
type: project
---

## Decision 1: Knowledge Graph + RAG over Pure RAG

**Chose:** Hybrid Knowledge Graph + RAG
**Rejected:** Pure vector RAG (already tried, failed)
**Why:** User's team already tried pure RAG and it couldn't understand inter-dependencies between requirements. Root cause analysis identified 5 failure modes:
1. No relationship awareness — chunks are independent, can't follow cross-doc dependencies
2. Undirected retrieval scope — searches entire corpus, may miss critical docs
3. Destroyed hierarchy — chunking loses parent-child structure that carries essential context
4. Poor telecom terminology handling — standard embeddings don't handle 3GPP acronyms well
5. Missing standards context — requirements that say "follow 3GPP TS X" are incomplete without standards text

**How to apply:** The graph is the routing layer that fixes problems 1-3. Contextualized chunks help with 4. Standards ingestion fixes 5.

## Decision 2: Graph Scoping → Targeted RAG (not Graph OR RAG)

**Chose:** Graph narrows scope first, then vector RAG ranks within that scope
**Rejected:** Graph-only retrieval, RAG-only retrieval, parallel graph+RAG
**Why:**
- Graph alone can't handle vague/conceptual queries ("what retry behavior is most aggressive?")
- Graph scoping may return too many nodes (30+), RAG helps rank them
- Graph may have edge gaps; RAG is the fallback
- RAG alone is what already failed
- The key insight: "graph tells us WHERE to look, RAG tells us WHAT's most relevant"

## Decision 3: Structural Parsing over LLM Extraction

**Chose:** Deterministic rule-based parser for document structure, LLM only for enrichment
**Rejected:** Pure LLM extraction of requirements and relationships
**Why:** VZW OA docs have highly consistent structure (requirement IDs follow `VZ_REQ_{PLANID}_{NUMBER}`, hierarchical section numbering, consistent zones). This structure is extractable with regex/rules — more reliable and cheaper than LLM extraction. LLM is reserved for what rules can't do: concept tagging, relationship classification, ambiguous cross-references.

## Decision 4: Standards Ingestion — Option C (Hybrid Selective)

**Chose:** Selectively ingest only referenced 3GPP sections + surrounding context (parent section, definitions, adjacent sub-sections)
**Rejected:**
- Option A (full ingestion of 3GPP specs) — too massive, TS 24.301 alone is 400+ pages
- Option B (only exact referenced sections) — misses context, a section may depend on adjacent definitions
**Why:** Option C is tractable for PoC (maybe 50-200 distinct section references across all VZW docs → ~5-10% of each standard), guarantees every ingested section is relevant, and includes enough surrounding context for completeness.

## Decision 5: Bottom-Up Feature Taxonomy Derivation

**Chose:** Derive taxonomy from documents using LLM, then human review
**Rejected:** Pre-defined taxonomy, purely manual taxonomy
**Why:** User explicitly chose to derive from documents first rather than imposing a predefined taxonomy. Approach: (1) LLM extracts features per document from TOC/section headings, (2) LLM consolidates across documents, (3) human reviews. This ensures the taxonomy reflects what's actually in the documents.

## Decision 6: Two Cross-Document Patterns

Identified two distinct patterns that need different graph structures:
- **Pattern (a) Fragmented:** One logical capability spans multiple docs (e.g., activation = SIM + UI + Network + Entitlement). No doc cross-references the others. Handled by Feature Nodes that group requirements across docs.
- **Pattern (b) Dependent:** Requirement X in Doc A is functionally incomplete without Requirement Y in Doc B (e.g., Data Retry behavior depends on SMS over IMS support). Handled by typed cross-document edges (depends_on, conditional_on, etc.).

## Decision 7: Standards Relationship Classification

Four relationship types between VZW requirements and 3GPP sections:
- **DEFER** — VZW says "follow 3GPP" with no modifications
- **CONSTRAIN** — VZW narrows an optional behavior
- **OVERRIDE** — VZW specifies different behavior from 3GPP default
- **EXTEND** — VZW adds beyond what 3GPP specifies

Delta summaries are pre-computed during ingestion so comparison queries don't need re-analysis.

## Decision 8: Keep RAG (Don't Skip to Context Stuffing)

I proposed potentially skipping vector RAG for PoC given the large context window and using graph scoping + context stuffing. User decided to keep RAG because:
- Production LLM context size may be much smaller than 2M
- Need to validate the full pipeline including vector retrieval

- Need to validate the full pipeline including vector retrieval
input.new_string:
## Decision 8: Keep RAG (Don't Skip to Context Stuffing)

I proposed potentially skipping vector RAG for PoC given the large context window and using graph scoping + context stuffing. User decided to keep RAG because:
- Production LLM context size may be much smaller than 2M
- Need to validate the full pipeline including vector retrieval

## Decision 9: Unified Graph + Vector Store over MxN Partitioned (2026-04-12)

**Chose:** Single unified graph and vector store spanning all MNOs and releases, with logical partitioning via node/chunk metadata (mno, release, doc_type)
**Rejected:** M (MNOs) × N (releases) separate graphs and vector stores
**Why:**
- Cross-MNO comparison queries are first-class requirements — unified graph makes these natural traversals, MxN requires external merge logic
- Standards nodes are shared across MNOs (both VZW and TMO reference 3GPP TS 24.301) — avoids duplication
- Feature nodes are shared across MNOs ("IMS Registration" links VZW and TMO requirements naturally)
- New MNO/release ingestion is additive — new nodes with new metadata, existing nodes untouched
- One query pipeline with metadata filters vs. routing to multiple stores and merging

**How to apply:** Every node carries `mno` and `release` attributes. Query-time filtering handles scoping. Production graph DB (Neo4j) indexed on these attributes for performance.

## Decision 10: Multi-MNO with Per-MNO Parser Registry (2026-04-12)

Each MNO has its own consistent document format for both requirements and test cases. Solution: parser registry pattern — each MNO gets a dedicated parser implementation. VZW parser built for PoC; TMO and ATT parsers added when those docs are available.

## Decision 11: Test Cases as First-Class Graph Citizens (2026-04-12)

Test case documents are ingested with their own structural parser (per MNO format), producing Test_Case nodes linked to Requirement nodes via `tested_by`/`tests` edges. Test cases also get embedded in the vector store with `doc_type=testcase` metadata for filtered retrieval.

## Decision 12: Release-Specific Standards Versioning (2026-04-12)

Different MNO releases may reference different releases of the same 3GPP spec (e.g., VZW references Release 10, TMO references Release 15 of TS 24.301). Each standards release becomes a separate Standard_Section node. This is critical for accurate cross-MNO comparison — same spec section may have different content at different releases.

## Decision 13: Folder-Structure-Driven Metadata (2026-04-12)

Source docs organized as `/<MNO>/<Release>/Requirements/` and `/<MNO>/<Release>/TestCases/`. The ingestion pipeline derives mno, release, and doc_type from the folder path — eliminates manual metadata entry and enforces consistency. Standards pre-downloaded into `/Standards/<Spec>/<Release>/`.
